evaluation_inputs.system_description: The Battlefield Control System (BCS) is a real-time system used by army battalions to control troop movement, strategy, and operations. It supports a Commander who commands a set of Soldiers and their equipment (weapons, sensors). The system interfaces with external command-and-control systems to receive commands and intelligence and to report status. It operates on a client-server model where the Commander node acts as the server and Soldier nodes are clients, communicating exclusively via encrypted messages over a shared 9600 baud radio modem channel.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Minimal; high level data flows and functionality divisions with no clear mapping to software constructs or architectural styles.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the structure of the message-handling software (modules, functions, APIs, layers)?
What facilities exist in the software architecture for self-testing and monitoring of software components?
What facilities exist for redundancy, liveness monitoring, failover, and data consistency maintenance?
What is the process/task view of the system, including mapping to hardware?
What functional dependencies exist among software components ('uses' view)?
What data is kept in the database, how big is it, how much does it change, and who reads/writes it?
What is the anticipated frequency and volume of data being transmitted among system components?

hardware_view_description: The Commander node acts as a server and the Soldier nodes are its clients.
Soldier nodes make requests of the Commander and update the server's database with their status.
Inter-node communication is only through encrypted messages sent via a radio modem.
The radio modem is a shared communication channel; only one node can broadcast at any moment.

questions: How is the failure of a component detected?
How is the failure of a communication channel detected?
What is the latency for a spare component to be turned into a working replacement?
By what means is a failed component marked for replacement?
How are the system's working components notified that they should use the services of the spare?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Same information presented to user, but different presentation (location, fonts, sizes, colors, etc.).
Additional data requested to be presented to user.
User requests a change of dialog.
A new device is added to the network, e.g., a location device that returns accurate GPS data.
An existing device adds additional fields that are not currently handled to existing messages.
The time budget for initialization is reduced from 5 minutes to 90 seconds.
Modem baud rate is increased by a factor of 4.
Can a new schedule be accommodated by the OS?.
Change the number of Soldier nodes from 25 to 35.
Change the number of simultaneous missions from 3 to 6.
A node converts from being a Soldier/client to become a Commander/server.

business_goals: Control movement, strategy, and operations of troops in real-time
Support a Commander commanding a set of Soldiers and their equipment (weapons, sensors)
Interface with numerous external command-and-control systems
technical_constraints: Must use a standard set of hardware and software
Communication via encrypted radio modem at 9600 baud (shared channel)
Extreme levels of system robustness required

architectural_approaches.description: The Commander node acts as the central server. Soldier nodes are clients. All communication occurs via encrypted messages over a shared 9600 baud radio modem channel.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralized command and data flow management can optimize coordination and resource allocation for real-time troop control.
The server (Commander node) is a critical single point; its design for reliability and potential failover directly impacts system uptime.
The model facilitates centralized enforcement of encryption and access control policies for all client communications.
Separation of concerns between server (command logic) and clients (soldier interfaces) can allow for independent updates to each side.

children.scenario_description: During a mission, the Commander issues a movement order to a squad of 10 Soldier nodes. The system must deliver the order and receive acknowledgments from all nodes within 2 seconds over the 9600 baud radio channel.

The primary Commander node fails due to hardware fault. A designated backup Commander node must assume control and restore full command capability to all Soldier nodes within 30 seconds without manual reconfiguration.
An adversary attempts to eavesdrop on the radio channel. The system must ensure all command, status, and data messages are encrypted such that intercepted traffic reveals no usable intelligence.

children.refinement: Update Soldier node software within 1 hour
children.scenario_description: A new communication protocol version is released. The system must allow remote, over-the-air update of the software on 50 Soldier nodes, deployed in the field, with less than 1 hour of total downtime per node.

scenarios.scenario_text: During a mission, the Commander issues a movement order to a squad of 10 Soldier nodes. The system must deliver the order and receive acknowledgments from all nodes within 2 seconds over the 9600 baud radio channel.

The primary Commander node fails due to hardware fault. A designated backup Commander node must assume control and restore full command capability to all Soldier nodes within 30 seconds without manual reconfiguration.
An adversary attempts to eavesdrop on the radio channel. The system must ensure all command, status, and data messages are encrypted such that intercepted traffic reveals no usable intelligence.

A new communication protocol version is released. The system must allow remote, over-the-air update of the software on 50 Soldier nodes, deployed in the field, with less than 1 hour of total downtime per node.

scenario_reference.text: During a mission, the Commander issues a movement order to a squad of 10 Soldier nodes. The system must deliver the order and receive acknowledgments from all nodes within 2 seconds over the 9600 baud radio channel.

analysis_records.stimulus: Commander issues a movement order to 10 Soldier nodes
analysis_records.response_measure: Deliver order and receive acknowledgments from all nodes within 2 seconds
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with Commander as central server
Communication via shared 9600 baud radio modem channel
analysis_records.reasoning: The 9600 baud channel is a severe bottleneck. A movement order and acknowledgments require serial transmission. With 10 nodes, the round-trip time for sequential acknowledgments may exceed 2 seconds, especially if messages are large or encryption adds overhead. The centralized server must manage all traffic, creating a single contention point.

risks.description: The 9600 baud channel bandwidth is insufficient to guarantee 2-second round-trip for 10 nodes with realistic message sizes and protocol overhead.
risks.rationale: Basic calculation: 9600 baud = 1200 bytes/sec. If a command is 100 bytes and an ACK is 50 bytes, total data per node is 150 bytes. For 10 nodes, that's 1500 bytes, requiring at least 1.25 seconds just for transmission, not including propagation, processing, encryption, and potential retransmissions. This likely exceeds the 2-second limit.

risks.description: Centralized server (Commander) serializes all communications, causing latency to accumulate as nodes wait for their turn on the shared channel.
risks.rationale: In a simple Client-Server model over a shared half-duplex channel, the server must poll or nodes must contend. This sequential handling adds significant delay, making the 2-second target for 10 nodes highly sensitive to message scheduling and collision avoidance.

non_risks.description: The Client-Server architecture itself provides a clear, managed communication path for command and control.
non_risks.rationale: The pattern is appropriate for centralized command. The risk is not the pattern choice but the extremely low bandwidth constraint combined with it.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of Soldier nodes in a squad receiving simultaneous orders

scenario_reference.text: The primary Commander node fails due to hardware fault. A designated backup Commander node must assume control and restore full command capability to all Soldier nodes within 30 seconds without manual reconfiguration.

analysis_records.stimulus: Hardware fault causes primary Commander node failure
analysis_records.response_measure: Backup Commander assumes control and restores full command capability to all Soldier nodes within 30 seconds
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with a single primary Commander server
Designated backup Commander node (implied by scenario)
analysis_records.reasoning: The Client-Server pattern creates a single point of failure (the Commander). The scenario assumes a backup exists. The 30-second recovery depends on failover mechanisms: backup detection of primary failure, state synchronization, and Soldier nodes reconnecting to the new server. Over a slow 9600 baud channel, broadcasting a new server identity and re-establishing sessions with all nodes could be time-consuming.

risks.description: Lack of detailed failover mechanism in the described architecture. How does the backup detect failure? How is state synchronized?
risks.rationale: The architectural approach only mentions a 'designated backup' in the scenario, not in the described approach. Without a defined heartbeat, lease, or state replication protocol, the 30-second recovery is not assured.

risks.description: Slow radio channel delays failover signaling and Soldier reconnection, risking the 30-second recovery window.
risks.rationale: Notifying 50+ Soldier nodes of a new Commander address and re-establishing encrypted sessions over 9600 baud will involve significant serial communication time.

non_risks.description: The concept of a designated backup server addresses the single-point-of-failure risk inherent in the Client-Server style.
non_risks.rationale: Having a standby server is a standard mitigation for availability in a centralized architecture.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Amount of session/state data to synchronize to backup

tradeoff_points.parameter: Frequency of state synchronization to backup

scenario_reference.text: An adversary attempts to eavesdrop on the radio channel. The system must ensure all command, status, and data messages are encrypted such that intercepted traffic reveals no usable intelligence.

analysis_records.response_measure: Intercepted traffic reveals no usable intelligence (all messages encrypted end-to-end)
architectural_decisions: All communication occurs via encrypted messages
analysis_records.reasoning: The architecture centralizes encryption policy at the server. This is effective for enforcing encryption. However, the slow channel may incentivize weak encryption to save bandwidth/processing time, creating a trade-off. Key distribution and management for all Soldier nodes is also a critical sub-problem.

risks.description: Encryption key management and distribution over the insecure, slow radio channel is challenging and could be a vulnerability.
risks.rationale: Initial key exchange or periodic key rotation must itself be secure. If compromised, encryption is void. The low bandwidth makes frequent key updates costly.

non_risks.description: The architectural decision to encrypt all messages addresses the confidentiality requirement against eavesdropping.
non_risks.rationale: Applying encryption to all traffic is a direct and necessary countermeasure for the stated threat.

scenario_reference.text: A new communication protocol version is released. The system must allow remote, over-the-air update of the software on 50 Soldier nodes, deployed in the field, with less than 1 hour of total downtime per node.

analysis_records.response_measure: Update software on 50 Soldier nodes remotely with <1 hour downtime per node
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with separation of concerns
analysis_records.reasoning: The separation allows Soldier node software to be updated independently. However, the 1-hour downtime limit is severely constrained by the 9600 baud channel. Transmitting a software image (likely several megabytes) to 50 nodes serially would take days. Parallel updates are limited by the shared channel. The architecture does not specify an update mechanism (e.g., broadcast, differential updates).

risks.description: The 9600 baud channel makes remote updates of any significant size to 50 nodes within 1 hour per node physically impossible if done serially or with limited parallelism.
risks.rationale: Example: A 10 MB update requires ~18.5 hours to transmit to one node at 1200 B/s. Even with 10:1 compression, it's ~1.85 hours, exceeding the limit before considering 49 other nodes.

risks.description: The Client-Server model may require the Commander to manage each update individually, creating a coordination and bandwidth management bottleneck.
risks.rationale: The server must potentially package, sign, and transmit unique updates to each client, overwhelming the low-bandwidth link.

non_risks.description: The separation of client and server logic supports independent updateability of Soldier node software in principle.
non_risks.rationale: The architectural style's modifiability benefit is valid, but the operational constraint (bandwidth) negates it in practice for this scenario.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Ability to use multicast/broadcast for update distribution
